data.title: Avadel Shareholders Wake Up To A Competing Bid For Their Shares
data.description:
Not only has biotech M&A activity picked up recently, but we now have at least two potentially contentious transactions in the space. Following Novo Nordisk A/S's (NVO) decision to fight Pfizer Inc. (PFE) for obesity drug developer Metsera, Inc. (MTSR), we now have Danish pharmaceutical company H. Lundbeck A/S (OTCPK:HLUBF) (OTCPK:HLBBF) (HLUN-A.CO) (HLUN-B.CO) coming in with a higher bid for Avadel Pharmaceuticals plc (AVDL) despite a standing agreement between this developer of narcolepsy drugs and Alkermes plc (ALKS).

With Lundbeck’s upfront offer already higher than the potential maximum of Alkermes’s offer (including the contingent value rights, or CVR), Avadel at a minimum must give serious consideration to Lundbeck’s bid. With recent competing clinical data suggesting that Avadel’s peak revenue potential may be at least a little higher than previously thought, there could be room for both bidders to go higher if need be, with both parties arguably needing the incremental revenue and profits that Avadel can offer.

We Have A New Bidder

Given that Avadel was essentially a one-product commercial-stage specialty pharma company without much of a pipeline, a buyout was widely regarded as a “when, not if” question, and that question got answered about a month ago when the company agreed to an all-cash deal with Alkermes.

Alkermes, looking to build out and secure its place in the narcolepsy treatment space, offered $18.50/share upfront and a CVR worth an additional $1.50/share tied to Avadel’s Lumryz getting FDA approval for idiopathic hypersomnia at some point before year-end ’28, and I’d say that particular condition is highly likely to be met (Phase III results will be out next year).

On November 14, and apparently without discussions with Avadel (which, I believe, would be precluded by the Alkermes buyout agreement), Lundbeck came in with an unsolicited rival bid for up to $23/share in cash—$21/share upfront and two $1/share CVRs tied to total sales of Lumryz through the end of 2030.

Thus far all parties are responding more or less the way most investors would expect – Avadel’s press release noted that Lundbeck’s bid was unsolicited but likely to be a superior offer, while Alkermes acknowledged the rival bid and said they are reviewing their options.

Unless I missed something in the proxy statement for Alkermes’s initial bid (and please feel free to respond in the comments if that’s the case), it doesn’t look like there’s a meaningful termination fee here – the proxy makes mention of Alkermes being entitled to 1% of the aggregate cash value of their bid, but that is effectively a trivial amount for Avadel/Lundbeck.

Why Lundbeck Would Want Avadel

Although Lundbeck is performing well right now, with third quarter revenue up a better-than-expected 14% and the company once again raising guidance for FY’25, Lundbeck is looking at some significant patent expirations in the coming years for Brintellix and Rexulti that could drive double-digit revenue declines after 2028. While there are some promising candidates in the pipeline, bexicaserin and amlenetug are higher-risk/higher-reward propositions and the pipeline otherwise skews heavily to early-stage (Phase I) compounds that won’t be ready in time to fill that gap.

Said differently, Lundbeck management is likely highly motivated to shore up the company’s revenue outlook in three to five years, and a specialty drug like Avadel’s Lumryz could fit the bill with peak sales potential likely between $500M and $1B.

I also believe this deal may have been prompted by a recent lackluster clinical data readout from Alkermes. The November 12 release of top-line Vibrance-2 data for alixorexton, a selective orexin2 receptor agonist for narcolepsy, showed efficacy that is good enough to get approved but not good enough to really differentiate from Lumryz (particularly when considering potential adverse events).

With that, the longer-term risk to Lumryz from competing OX2R agonists (including compounds from Alkermes, Takeda Pharmaceutical Company Limited (TAK), and Centessa Pharmaceuticals plc (CNTA) don’t seem quite as formidable now, and the odds of Lumryz hitting management’s long-term target of $1B in sales (roughly one-third to half of the addressable market) seem a fair bit better, particularly when factoring in the potential of Lumryz in the idiopathic hypersomnia market.

What Happens Next?

Lundbeck isn’t alone in needing to shore up its intermediate-term revenue growth, as current sell-side expectations call for Alkermes to see a dip in revenue in 2027 (it’s unclear to me how many, if any, estimates in 2027 already add in Avadel revenue). Moreover, it’s plausible that Lumryz could prove to be synergistic with alixorexton, making this a “franchise-building” deal that would provide outsized benefits to Alkermes.

Said differently, both Lundbeck and Alkermes have good reasons to want Avadel. If this becomes a battle of resources, I would generally favor Alkermes – they ended the last quarter with around $1.1B in net cash versus the $1.3B in net debt at Lundbeck. While Lundbeck may arguably appear more desperate given a steeper upcoming revenue cliff, Alkermes has already poured significant resources into their narcolepsy program and may feel that Avadel is key to generating good returns from those investments (particularly with the Vibrance-2 top-line read).

While bidding wars are typically only good for sellers, there is still some room to go on Avadel before the price becomes problematic. Looking at comparable deals over the last two years (deals for smaller commercial-stage biopharma), premiums average out to around 4.5x to 5.5x peak revenue estimates. Prior to the Vibrance-2 read-out, the average sell-side estimate for 2032 revenue for Lumryz was $720M, so even this new Lundbeck deal ($2.4B including the CVRs) offers “headroom” for higher bids.

Obviously this is a crude way to approach valuation – peak sales estimates are notoriously inaccurate the further out you have to go, and the comparable transactions all had different timelines to peak revenue (buying an asset expected to peak in 2028 is quite a bit different from one that will peak post-2032). Still, I think it’s at least useful as a framing mechanism for where the bids could go.

The Bottom Line

As a Lundbeck shareholder, I’m fine with the deal that the company offered, and I believe they could offer a little more and still come out ahead (including not unduly stretching the balance sheet). I see it as a logical bid, and unlike many of Lundbeck’s past ill-fated M&A offers, there’s far less clinical risk here – with the value skewed more toward sales execution, I think this could be a good deal for the company.

For Alkermes, it really comes down to how they think alixorexton can fare in the market with the data in hand and how much more they want to spend to build their narcolepsy franchise. As I said before, while Lundbeck may feel more pressure to do a deal to sustain revenue growth, Alkermes likely has more dry powder in a bidding war.

Last and not least is Avadel. That the company will be sold is a virtual guarantee now, so if I were an Avadel shareholder, I’d sit back and enjoy a process that could boost my profits even further. At a minimum, I see no reason to be quick to sell until/unless one of these competing bidders bows out.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD: Big Numbers Will Need Flawless Execution To Not Disappoint
data.description:
AMD: Aiming To Take Market Share Against Nvidia?

Second fiddle to the AI race? Well, the key takeaways from the recent investor day held by Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) have clearly underscored that this race to define the multitrillion-dollar AI buildout is only just getting started. Sure, Advanced Micro Devices is still firmly in second place, with a single-digit market share in AI chips right now, as compared to Nvidia's (NVDA) dominance.

However, the company has laid it all out and is upping the ante in this battle royal against Nvidia. AMD believes that gunning for "double-digit" market share is in the making, and relatively speaking, that suggests relative share losses from Nvidia's vantage point. Aspirational might be what Nvidia bulls will remind us, and the race remains Nvidia's to lose until AMD can close the gap between execution and rhetoric, I believe.

Yet, AMD shares have not only outperformed NVDA since early October, it has also led the broad market as well. I don't expect Nvidia to lose a firm grip as the primary AI arms dealer anytime soon, but it also goes to show that the burgeoning AI infra is too monolithic to be dominated solely by one supplier. Not only does it compound substantive concentration risks, but it might also lead to complacency by that one supplier.

However, if Nvidia's annualized product refresh cadence for its much-vaunted AI chips is anything to go by, Nvidia's competitive instincts are still expected to stay resolute, and that should keep AMD and custom XPU leader Broadcom (AVGO) heaping more pressure as they bid to follow closely behind while attempting to close the daylight between them and NVDA CEO Jensen Huang's throne.

In my last AMD update, I enunciated why the chip partnership with OpenAI (OPEN) is expected to be transformational. Not just in name, but also helping Dr. Lisa Su and her team to gain a credible foothold in the eyes of the market. I guess that has served AMD investors well in the past month or so, given its breathtaking performance. The semiconductor leader then followed up with a timely investor day by affording the market even more clarity into its growth pathway toward 2030. And, if you have given some thoughts about the metrics promulgated by management, aren't numbers that you will usually consider as insignificant?

In fact, if I thought that the announcement with OpenAI CEO Sam Altman heralded AMD's entry onto the big stage, the investor event this week provided assessable guideposts that we can make references to when discussing AMD's growth prospects and whether it could make a dent in Nvidia's leadership.

AMD: Road To A $1 Trillion TAM

Data center will continue to underpin AMD's ascendance through the next five years, in a similar fashion that saw the company took center stage in its competition with Intel (INTC) in CPUs. Management reminds us that data center comprised 17% of its business in 2020 but are expected to scale to 47% of total revenue by the end of 2025, surpassing $16B. Yet, if we think about Nvidia's print for the July quarter, where data center revenue is almost $147B over the past 12 months. That compares with just under $15B in the October 2022 quarter, when data center accounted for just 51%. Now, it's 89%!

Hence, I believe Nvidia's success has provided a runway of sorts for AMD to kind of emulate, so to speak, as the market will, without a doubt, look past investor conferences to validate AMD's roadmap in the next few years.

The numbers presented in AMD's recent event are nothing short of stunning. From a TAM of $500B by 2028, management underscores the plausibility of seeing it double to $1T through 2030. AMD's confidence in seeing 80% CAGR in data center revenue isn't an anomaly, as I highlighted earlier, with Nvidia providing a blueprint for success.

Hence, those "grandiose" numbers should help AMD print an overall revenue growth in excess of 35% (while contemplating the slower core business growing by 10% CAGR). AMD's earnings multiple will arguably be predicated on an emerging growth story within the AI chips backdrop but underpinned by its already profitable core segments that should help the company quickly achieve scale advantages.

AMD: Margins Should Scale Higher

Wall Street is wasting little time, as they updated their AMD modeling, taking into account these exciting prospects right from the 2026 fiscal year. As more capacity is expected to come online from 2027, it should manifest in the P&L more profoundly two years from now. That timeline seems reasonable and realistic enough, allowing more time for us to ascertain whether OpenAI is gaining increasingly closer to hitting its reported positive FCF numbers by the end of the decade. Since operating leverage will be a key narrative behind these reported numbers, AMD's ability to execute will be front and center, aligning closely with the market scrutiny on OpenAI's roadmap.

That said, building this unprecedented scale of AI clusters/factories isn't going to be as simple as a click of a button. CoreWeave's (CRWV) recent supply chain snafu should have informed us that steep execution risks exist and could stun us in unexpected ways that could be hard to anticipate ahead of time. And for AMD, while the announcement with OpenAI is laudable, it's, after all, just another partnership that the AI company inked with archrivals like Nvidia and Broadcom. Altman isn't going to moor his company's fate to that of AMD just yet, even though OpenAI has much to gain based on the AMD warrants it owns if required milestones are subsequently attained.

Nevertheless, as compared to Oracle (ORCL), AMD is an arms dealer through and through (not a hyperscaler). And unlike Oracle, AMD supplies the chips for OpenAI to build. Hence, negative FCF will be much less of a headwind as implied in AMD's solid FCF margins trajectory, expected to hit >20% by FY2027.

As AMD advances up the rack scale solutions stack, these more highly prized full-stack opportunities should help command a sizable premium as builders also seek to diversify their roadmap against Nvidia. Hence, AMD investors wouldn't need to think so much about seeking external funding that the builders will need to acquire, allowing Lisa Su and her team to concentrate on closing the disconnect with Nvidia's data center revenue in the next few years. Given the immense opportunities available to the firm, it doesn't even need to unseat Nvidia to actualize the potential.

Buy Or Sell AMD Stock?

With the recent surge in AMD shares, the valuation bifurcation against NVDA has widened, while also trading at a palpable premium against its 5Y average. AMD's forward EBITDA multiple of >43x underscores the optimism when we consider that against NVDA's 26x multiple.

Hence, I think we could very well argue that the market has kind of reflected quite a fair bit of confidence in AMD's build cadence and possibly also given more than its fair share of the benefit of the doubt. If I'm you, I will definitely be more careful, as the market could quickly digest the gaps, as the aggressiveness in AMD's metrics could impinge on its ability to dish out consistently solid beats-and-raises in the next couple of years to justify another bigger valuation upgrade.

We might have to afford more time for the recent surge to be digested first, which may come sooner than expected, as the market appears to be reallocating from growth to value as I pen this update, while also letting out air from the speculative stocks.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: LG Display Is Turning Its Biggest Problem Into Its Greatest Strength
data.description:
Thesis

LG Display Co., Ltd. (NYSE:LPL) is a company from South Korea that makes screens, the kind you see on TVs, smartphones, computers, and even car dashboards. It’s part of the bigger LG Group, the same company known for making LG TVs, like the one in my home, maybe yours too, along with home appliances. They’re especially famous for two types of screen technology, one's called OLED, which stands for “organic light-emitting diode,” and it’s known for making really bright, colorful images with deep contrast, that’s why OLED TVs look so good.

LG Display makes a version of this called “WOLED,” which many other big TV brands buy from them to use in their own televisions. The other type is called LCD, which stands for “liquid crystal display.” That’s a more traditional kind of screen most people are familiar with, since it's used in lots of devices. And in addition to TVs, LG Display also creates smaller screens for devices like phones, tablets, laptops, and smartwatches. These are known as AMOLED screens.

As for the company’s stock, it's up 36.22% over six months and 43.32% year to date, well ahead of the S&P 500 (SP500).

After going over the latest earnings, my research argues that LPL, after years of losses and restructuring, have now given way to a leaner business model that’s worth the risk of entry.

LPL's Q3 2025 Financial Highlights

In the third quarter, LG Display pulled in roughly 6.96 trillion Korean won, that’s about 25% higher than the quarter before and 2% above where they stood a year earlier. The driver here, unsurprisingly, was OLED, specifically. The price difference between those screens and the others wasn't big, but it gave them more selling power, which is what really helped their sales.

Operating profit came in at 431 billion won, though that figure includes a one-off 40 billion won hit from a staff restructuring. Strip that out, and you’re talking closer to 470 billion won. The company only made a small profit, about 1.2 billion won, and most of that came from currency exchange benefits. Basically, since the Korean won was weak, the money they made from exports looked higher when changed back into won.

EBITDA came to 1.42 trillion won, which means they earned about 20% profit. The total screen area they shipped was down about 1% from the previous quarter, mainly because they produced fewer low-cost LCD screens. However, at $1,365 a square meter, prices reached a record as the profit curve continues to bend toward OLED.

You can see the change in what they’re selling: mobile panels, the ones used in phones and wearable, rose to 39% of revenue. IT products such as monitors and laptops slipped a little to 37%, while TV panels fell to 16% and car displays dropped to 8%. Overall, OLED products made up 65% of total sales.

They currently have around 1.56 trillion won in cash. Their debt has gone down a bit, and the ratio of debt to equity has improved. This means their financial situation is getting better, especially now that the money-losing LCD business is being phased out.

I wanted to pump the brakes, just for a moment, to rewind the timeline to give readers a little context. Going back to 2020, LG Display announced it would stop making LCD TVs in Korea and focus instead on OLED screens, looking to get about half of its revenue from OLED within a few years. By 2024, it took the last step, selling its Guangzhou LCD plant to TCL and using the proceeds to double down on OLED.

As things stands, on the conference call, management stressed that the OLED turn is finally translating into real-world results. Suk Heo, Head of IR Division & Director, explained:

As we continue to expand the performance of OLED-centric business structure upgrade, its impact is further solidifying our foundation for growth and profitability.

CFO Sung-Hyun Kim added:

Q3 this year was when we saw the results of our ongoing strategy to upgrade our business structure to be more OLED-centric and our strong initiatives for cost innovation beginning to come to fruition and manifest themselves into business performance.

From January to September, the company made 18.6 trillion won in revenue and earned 345 billion won in operating profit. That’s about a one-trillion-won improvement from last year, a big turnaround, you can see for yourselves in the operating income line above, for a company that’s been wading through red ink.

In the meantime, the CFO also signaled another, larger round of workforce changes for the next quarter, aimed squarely at trimming costs, a move they expect to pay off in about 18 months. Capital spending still remains high, but, they think, that'll stay below last year’s level, around 1 trillion won.

LPL's Business Environment And Guidance

As far as the broader picture goes, in October, South Korea’s exports went up by about 3.6% compared to the same month last year. But global shipping delays, new tariffs, and general economic instability have made it hard for businesses, like LG Display, to make plans with trust. Management's CFO Sung-Hyun Kim was careful to point that out on the earnings call:

External uncertainties and the consequent shipment volatility are expected to persist in Q4. There still remain variables in the business environment, including macro-related real demand, intensifying competition among suppliers and supply chain stability.

Then there’s the pricing. LCD screens keep getting cheaper, almost collapsing under their own ubiquity. That’s painful for the firms still betting on them, but oddly enough, it works in LG Display’s favor. As LCD prices sink, OLED panels, brighter, thinner, and far pricier, start to look like a rational upgrade. In areas like gaming displays, that perspective change is already obvious. LG Display has been leaning into it, pushing its so-called Tandem OLED technology.

Looking at the company’s performance by segment, the small mobile unit, the one that makes screens for phones and wearables, did well. Shipments climbed more than 20% in the first half of the year, and profits got better later on as more of those panels were used in premium devices.

A big part of that demand came from their largest customer in North America, although the mix could change depending on which phone models sell the most. LG Display also seems to have strengthened its control over the smartwatch supply chain, but they haven’t shared any specific numbers about it.

Meanwhile, in the medium-sized display, products are slowly becoming more profitable. The IT LCD side is staying narrow, sticking to B2B clients, while OLED is reserved for high-end tablets and new laptops, growth only if the market asks for it.

While over in the large-panel division, the one that supplies TVs and gaming screens, that's still LG Display’s flagship business. The company says it expects to ship around six million large OLED panels this year and about seven million next year. And more of those panels are now being used for high-end gaming monitors, since they use similar production methods. As for capacity, LG Display’s factories can make up to 180,000 large OLED sheets a month, and they’re currently running at about 135,000, so it looks like there’s plenty of room to increase production, assuming, that's going to be needed in the near future.

LPL's Valuation

At $4.40 per share, the stock's trading at about 16x its blended P/E ratio, compared to a typical or “fair” P/E of 13.5.

Based on expected earnings, the fair value forecast is modeling an extremely generous, in my opinion, estimate at almost $13 per share. I've said this before, and it's worth repeating, I always take these metrics with a heavy grain of salt. Even so, the data takes it much higher when factoring in that if earnings grow as projected and the P/E ratio returns to its usual level, LPL could reach ~$30, an annualized gain of roughly 46%. Let's set that aside and move on.

In the short-term reality, sales are growing very slowly, up just 1.01% year over year (graded D+) and expected to rise 4.32% going forward.

After revenue growth, another whiff of strength comes from that operating leverage I showed earlier, EBITDA is up 7.13% year over year and is projected to jump 40.81% in the future.

Cash flow is another swing factor here, as operating cash flow has climbed 38.61% year over year and is expected to grow another 39.63% going forward. The takeaway from these points is that if those numbers hold, they provide solid support for the P/E-based valuation story.

But right now, I'd call for some caution about how those future earnings will actually materialize. The stock’s forward P/E for 2025 is 8.32, which looks cheap, but by next year, that same data shows earnings are expected to drop by about 84%, pushing the implied P/E up to 51.12, a classic warning sign, just maybe, when you’re judging value based on P/E ratios.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I plan on investing in the OLED-led turnaround while the stock still trades at a discounted 2025 forward P/E. Earnings and cash flow are starting to turn up, and the ongoing pullback in LCD should improve margins. I realize that making a "buy" call as the market shows a second straight day of triple-digit losses, especially in tech, comes with a very high risk of softer earnings in 2026 and the usual global headwinds. But risk is risk, and I'm not taking a big position here, just basing it on solid OLED execution that could make it worth it over the longer term.

data.title: A Runaway Meltdown In The Cooking: Lower Rates Won't Solve Unemployment
data.description:
I’ve been bullish on an increase in unemployment since the last nonfarm payroll data came out back in September.

My rationale is (annoyingly) simple.

I have a strong conviction that the unemployment rate print (either for September, October, or November) will be hot, based on the private data available. The Challenger report showed 153,074 job cuts in October, up 183% sequentially, and YTD it shows the highest job cut announcements since 2020. Corporations are announcing layoffs, mainly driven by AI adoption.

On the inflation side, I have an even stronger conviction that the CPI and PCE will come in softer than anticipated (just like the September reading, when inflation was 3% vs. 3.1% consensus). I see a deflationary environment in transportation and housing, which, historically, have been the main contributors to inflation. Furthermore, tariffs are not leading to a meaningful increase in inflation.

I strongly believe that hot unemployment and moderately cold inflation will support a further rate cut in December. This will likely remove an overhang after the end of the shutdown, given that the markets are currently pricing in a rate cut at a similar chance as a coin flip.

Furthermore, despite the remarks from the White House that the October data may never be released, I already see Director of the National Economic Council Kevin Hassett hinting that, at least, partial data could be released. Overall, I would expect October/November CPI to come in anywhere between 3% and 3.2% and unemployment to increase from the last reading in August (4.3% to 4.5%).

In the broader scheme of things, I strongly believe rate cuts will not solve unemployment. I project this will lead to a runaway meltdown, where the Fed aggressively lowers the rates to counter unemployment, without much success. I project that will lead to a material bull run in the markets due to the increase in liquidity, right before a crash, as consumer spending will eventually catch up with reality, hitting the hyperscalers (and their CapEx plans). Until then, I reaffirm my strong buy rating.

Macro Recap After The End Of The US Gov. Shutdown

As we leave behind the end of the shutdown, I see a few overhangs that are pressing investor confidence. The main one is the statement from the White House that October's inflation and unemployment data are likely to never be released.

Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell previously warned about the risk of missing the October data.

If this goes on for a while, they won’t be collecting it. And it could become more challenging

From what I'm reading, the Fed officials are increasingly hawkish after the shutdown. Here are the remarks from the Boston Fed president, Susan Collins, on November 13:

Absent evidence of a notable labor market deterioration, I would be hesitant to ease policy further, especially given the limited information on inflation due to the government shutdown

As seen below, Polymarket has drastically reduced the odds of another rate cut after the White House remarks on the October economic data.

The CME Group is also reflecting a significant drop in the odds of another rate cut in December.

Now, it seems that Hassett is positive on a partial release of the delayed economic data for October, with expectations of high unemployment coupled with high inflation.

Even though I see a conflict of opinions, with White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt saying data is likely to never be released and Hassett noting that partial data could be published, I have a high conviction that (partial) economic data for October will eventually be published. Why?

As a market participant, I have gained a feel for how to interpret remarks from the US Government. When I see a strong statement (for example, that October economic data is likely never to be released) followed by a quick sign of backing off (for example, Hassett saying that partial data could be released), I lean to the positive side.

That said, plenty of key US economic indicators have been delayed, and not all matter for the market. Below, I walk through the three that I believe are the most important ones.

The Consumer Price Index

This was the only indicator released during the US shutdown, as the September CPI data had already been fully collected before funding lapsed. The rationale for the release was to calculate the Social Security cost of living adjustment, which affects tens of millions of beneficiaries and has strict statutory deadlines.

The CPI for September showed an inflation rate of 3% (all items), with gasoline prices (4.1%) as the main driver for the increase.

Contrary to what many believed (mainly due to the impact of tariffs), the CPI reading wasn't hot. The consensus was expecting a reading of 3.1% prior to the release.

The chart below shows the evolution of CPI for the past 6 years. Overall, I see an increase this year, although the broader trend since 2023 is downwards.

The Personal Consumption Expenditures

I prefer this indicator over the CPI to gauge where inflation is, as it is a more realistic representation of what households are actually buying.

The latest data we have for the PCE is the August release, when the index increased 2.7% yoy (the highest in six months).

As seen above, the evolution of the PCE closely tracks the CPI, so the difference is not abysmal. However, when I see a divergence between the two, I tend to prioritize the PCE reading over the CPI.

In regard to the Fed, I believe a good reason why they paused interest rate cuts between the end of last year and September this year is due to the fear that Trump's tariffs could lead to a meaningful increase in inflation.

As seen in the CPI reading for September, the impact was minimal, below what the market anticipated. I expect the reading for the month of October (if it gets eventually published) and November to be in line with the September data, anywhere between 3% and 3.2%. Why?

The two main drivers for inflation are housing and transportation. I expect to see deflation for both of them. The Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index showed a 2% sequential decline for the month of October, with no change since October 2024. Furthermore, the national median rent dipped for the third consecutive month in October (-0.8% yoy).

Overall, I believe that the inflation reading will not be hot in October/November, which will set the Fed up for another rate cut in December if the jobs data is supportive.

The Employment Situation Report

Here, the latest data we have is for the month of August. The last reading showed an increase in the unemployment rate to 4.3%

The Challenger report noted the following remarks in its latest release:

October’s pace of job cutting was much higher than average for the month. Some industries are correcting after the hiring boom of the pandemic, but this comes as AI adoption, softening consumer and corporate spending, and rising costs drive belt-tightening and hiring freezes.

The data showed the highest YTD job cut announcements since 2020.

Looking at the ADP report, I see a mixed picture in September and October. In September, private sector employment fell by 32,000 jobs, just to rebound in October after an increase of 42,000 jobs.

After the latest unemployment data was released, I published a piece titled "Ultra Bullish On Unemployment Ticking Up To 4.3% In August."

It is my strongest conviction that the adoption of AI is leading to a deterioration in the US jobs market, even though there are some skeptics who believe the "AI justification" is being used as an excuse to right-size after the post-pandemic hiring glut.

Risks

There are a few material risks to my bull case. First, regardless of where the unemployment rate is, if the inflation reading is hot, I would expect the Fed to hesitate on a rate cut in December. As I said before, I see a deflationary landscape in transportation and housing. However, I can be wrong, and other items, especially goods, could drive inflation if corporations pass the tariffs to consumers.

Another risk could be the lack of October data due to the shutdown. If all the Fed has is the September and November data, I believe hesitation to lower rates could increase.

Finally, from a technical perspective, the chart below is not particularly attractive.

If yesterday's selloff doesn't find a bottom in the next few days, I would anticipate the downward pressure to accelerate going into the December Fed meeting.

Conclusion

Let me be clear: no matter how much Powell lowers the interest rate, I believe unemployment will not improve past a certain point. Why?

I see more and more corporations stating that they are planning to grow revenue with the same (or even less) headcount. I believe this could lead to a runaway meltdown, where the Fed lowers the rate aggressively, with minimal effect on the jobs market.

The lower rates will add liquidity to the markets, fueling a bull run right before a massive crash begins. I'm willing to ride both waves (the way up and down). To be direct, I don't expect the selloff to happen within the next 12 months, as the Fed has traditionally been slow with the interest rate cuts. Even if Powell is replaced by a dovish chair, I still believe it would take at least a year for the runaway meltdown to take place.

data.title: Red Cat Misses Estimates, Expands Operations
data.description:
Thesis

Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (RCAT) for Q3 25 reported an EPS loss of about –$0.16, missing expectations by $0.07. Elsewhere, revenue came in at $9.65 million, well below the $14.12 million consensus. The market reaction obviously wasn't good, and shares are down roughly 12% since the press release. The company also guided Q4 '25 revenue to be between $20 and 23 million, short of some analysts’ $26.4 million estimate.

The good news, despite the miss on the figures, was that Red Cat still hold a pretty decent cash position. They are also seeing some robust demand across their Black Widow platform and have had to double the production space to deliver on contracts sooner. The biggest contract is the U.S. Army’s SRR Tranche 2 contract.

With regards to their defense portfolio, things are also expanding, with the company expected to ramp up capabilities in their new marine time division, which we will get to shortly. Post earnings, I see this stock as being very cheap, now trading in the $7 range. It's a big pull-back from the $15 we saw back in mid-October, and I think there should be a recovery, but the company needs time to deliver on contracts.

As per my previous coverage, I was Buy-rated going into earnings, and I have to say I got it wrong. I didn’t anticipate the miss, and my buy call is down 27% as a result. However, I still like the direction the company is heading in and think it is still early days until a wider portfolio of contract revenue can boost those earnings figures. I remain buy-rated despite the miss and think we should see more upside to come post selloff.

A miss on earnings

Since earnings, the stock has been on a slow decline, down about 14% since the announcement. For Q3 '25, the good news I saw was that there was some explosive top-line momentum, mainly driven by the U.S. Army’s SRR UAS Tranche 2 program. We also saw some good growth from the company’s attempt to rapidly scale up across both drone and maritime systems. Revenue has actually surged about 646% year over year and a hefty 200% sequentially to $9.6 million. It’s a figure that beat expectations, and management even raised full-year revenue guidance to $34.5 to $37.5 million, with Q4 alone projected to fall somewhere between $20 and $23 million.

The balance sheet also transformed dramatically: Red Cat managed to end the quarter with roughly $212.5 million in cash and receivables. Yes, this sum was driven largely by its recent equity raise, but it should provide ample liquidity to expand manufacturing capacity, and more importantly, see out execution on the SRR contract.

Operationally, the company is trying to push itself towards a multi-domain unmanned systems provider, and I thought they’re going in the right direction. We saw the launch of Blue Ops this quarter, which is their newly launched maritime division, aimed at building uncrewed surface vessels (USVs). To support this, there was the opening of a 155,000 sq. ft. USV facility. Elsewhere, the company doubled the manufacturing space for Teal and FlightWave, and managed to get some pretty key certifications and partnerships throughout the quarter (NATO NSPA listing, Blue UAS clearance, Palantir VNav testing, AeroVironment integration). These all show me a business aggressively preparing for scale.

Now, despite this strong top-line trajectory and that major bump up in liquidity, we have to mention the market’s reaction. Since earnings, there’s been a roughly 14% drop in the stock, suggesting some investor concern around profitability/execution risk. Red Cat posted a $16.0 million net loss in Q3 and more than $52 million in losses year-to-date. Revenue coming in at just $9.6 million was also a bit off Wall Street's estimates of $14.12 million. Operating expenses also continued to grow ahead of revenue as the company ramps up R&D, manufacturing, and commercialization capabilities.

And whilst the substantial equity raise strengthened the balance sheet, it also significantly increased the share count, which some investors clearly weren't too happy about. Investors might also be weighing up the sustainability of government-driven demand going into 2026, and also some of the risks associated with scaling across both air and sea drones simultaneously, which we will get to shortly. So the fundamentals show some accelerating adoption and strategic progress, but there is no denying that the path to profitability, integration of new business lines, and consistency in revenue execution are still the key questions weighing on the stock in the near term.

A ramp-up in activity

During the quarter, I thought Red Cat made some pretty significant advances in expanding both portfolio breadth and depth. So they now offer more types of drone platforms and sometimes more than one drone choice within a platform. This kind of optionality is going to be key moving forward. If there’s one thing the U.S. Army or even NATO likes, it’s competition. They encourage contract competition, and if Red Cat have a drone or USV to compete more widely across the competition space, it's a good sign.

On that note, we saw the company launch its maritime division, Blue Ops. They opened a 155,000 sq ft facility in Georgia with the capacity to build more than 500 USVs per year.As a quick reminder, the USVs are modular, already combat-tested, uncrewed surface vessels designed for things like ISR, electronic warfare, and even multi-domain missions. They have autonomous operation capabilities built in and also manned-unmanned teaming capabilities. The company is tipped to make its first five prototype vessels through a partnership with Hodgdon Shipbuilding and has opened a new Georgia facility to scale production on that front.

Now, whilst the USVs appear to support advanced autonomy and integrated sensor intelligence, it’s still early days for Blue Ops, and they have not yet announced any major contract awards. I see the USVs as an emerging but strategically important growth area for Red Cat that could become a key catalyst come 2026.

At the same time, Red Cat this past quarter managed to double their manufacturing space for its drone operations. A good sign of robust demand. The drones are mainly built in Salt Lake City (Teal) and Los Angeles (FlightWave), and management mentioned they need the space to support ramping delivery of its Black Widow drone system under the U.S. Army’s SRR Tranche 2 contract. The Black Widow is, of course, one of their best sellers and the current contract is now expected to expand to about $35 million. The contract is milestone-based, and the sooner Red Cat get the shipments out, the sooner they can collect on revenue.

Execution risk

Now, whilst Red Cat’s earnings report highlighted some major contract advances, such as the expanded U.S. Army SRR UAS Tranche 2 order, which is now valued at approximately $35 million, it also comes with some significant execution risk. Given the company’s rapid scale-up, which includes doubling drone manufacturing capacity and also launching a massive 155,000 sq ft USV facility, delays or cost overruns in drone production could be quite impactful on its ability to deliver on these contracts. A delay in drone delivery would not be taken too lightly by the market and would see some downside for the stock. For example, they have a Q4 '25 guidance set at $20 to 23 million, so anything short of the projected contract fulfilment timeline would push the revenue recognition until the quarter after.

Valuation

Red Cat’s valuation profile, even with the post-earnings sell-off, remains pretty expensive relative to the broader sector. The forward EV to sales of 16.15x and forward price to sales of 16.89x are each more than 350% above the sector medians.

So, with all the product lines they have going on, the market is still pricing in substantial future growth and contract execution. The company’s price to book of 8.82x also screens high at more than 138% above the sector median, which suggests investors are still paying a significant premium to any tangible equity. So with no meaningful earnings or cash-flow multiples to anchor valuation, Red Cat’s pricing appears to be driven almost entirely by expectations for future defense revenue ramp-up. It’s a combination that makes the stock highly sensitive to any near-term signs of execution risk or delays on drone shipments.

Looking forward

However, the one positive thing I see about the pullback is that the stock is now extremely cheap, trading in the $7 range. Back in mid-October, we saw shares run up into the mid-$15 range, followed by this pullback. In fact, the stock recovered so well post Liberation Day that this strong sell-off seems inevitable. Shares were up a maximum of 218% since April 22nd.

data.title: Rakuten: Q3 Beat And Positive Corporate Developments Draw Attention
data.description:
I remain bullish on Rakuten Group, Inc. (OTCPK:RKUNY) (OTCPK:RKUNF) (4755.T).

The company's third-quarter results were outstanding. I'm positive on its partnership with HP Inc. (HPQ) and the potential IPO of its credit card operations.

RKUNY's better-than-expected 2Q25 EBITDA and the narrowing of its "Holding Company" discount were detailed in my previous August 12, 2025, article.

Operating Income More Than Tripled In Recent Quarter

The firm's financials for Jul-Sep '25 were released on Thursday, Nov. 13.

RKUNY recorded a 3Q2025 "operating profit" or "OP" of ¥39 billion. Based on S&P Capital IQ data, that exceeded the sell-side's projection by +65%.

The company's OP surged 213% in the latest three-month period on year-on-year terms. All three of its segments did well.

The "FinTech" unit saw its profit rise 38% YoY to ¥55 billion for the 3rd quarter of the current year.

Its results announcement highlighted "efforts to expand the customer base and transaction value in each service" provided by "the FinTech segment." As per the chart presented below, this division was able to deliver a meaningful increase in users and business activity across the board.

RKUNY's Credit Card, Banking, And Brokerage Sub-Segment Metrics

Separately, the "Internet Services" operations benefited from both internal and external tailwinds.

An Overview Of Japan's "Furusato Nozei" Or "Hometown Tax Donation" Program

"The Japan Times" reported in Sep that "website operators are holding events and campaigns to lure customers before the (Japanese) government bans them from giving reward points to donors, effective next month (Oct)." The scheme, which has been phased out, is outlined in the chart above.

Revenue for RKUNY's e-commerce business, or "Rakuten Ichiba" increased 10% YoY in 3Q25. This was driven by pull-forward demand from Japan's consumers seeking to take advantage of tax incentives.

Also, RKUNY shared at its analyst briefing (source: S&P Capital IQ) that "we have been offering benefits (to Rakuten Mobile subscribers) that make Rakuten Ichiba more attractive." One example is "early access" for certain "sales campaigns."

The proportion of Ichiba's "monthly active users" or "MAUs" who are also MNO clients went up from 3Q24's 14.4% to 3Q25's 16.2%.

My mid-Aug piece indicated that it "has done a great job cross-selling its internet-related products and services to the MNO (Mobile Network Operations) users." This strategy is clearly working.

Lastly, RKUNY's mobile/wireless operating loss shrunk from -¥51 billion in the 3rd quarter last year to -¥37 billion for 3Q2025. This can be explained by enhanced fixed-cost leverage effects.

This segment's profitability is a function of scale economies. Its wireless subscriber base has already reached the 9.5 million (+17% YoY) mark, which comes very close to its year-end goal of 10 million.

RKUNY is anticipating a ¥0.4 trillion EBITDA for FY25, implying a solid 23% growth. That should be doable, given that the actual 9M25 figure is already equivalent to 76% of the full-year management estimate.

Eyes On Card Business Listing And Collaboration With PC Maker

There are other things I like about RKUNY, apart from the profit beat.

A re-rating trigger to watch is the potential "initial public offering in the United States of its credit card business" as per the mid-Oct SA report.

I think this will be good for both card-related operations and the company as a whole, if it does materialize.

RKUNY aims to have its domestic credit card market share increase from "24.6%" as of end-2024 to "30%" in the mid-to-long term, according to a May 8, 2025, blog post. My Jan '24 write-up cited research from Deutsche Bank (DB) suggesting that Japan's "cashless payment penetration rate" may double to "80% in the long run."

Funds raised from an IPO will enable "Rakuten Card" to capitalize on the cash-to-card transition.

Also, RKUNY's "leverage" or "net debt-to-EBITDA" was still elevated at 8.3 times as of Sep 30, 2025, even though it's down substantially from 11.7x at the end of last year.

It is targeting for the "leverage" ratio to drop to 6x by end-2026. Monies coming in from a listing can be used to repay borrowings.

Another area of focus is AI-related opportunities.

Earlier on Nov. 11, RKUNY disclosed that "Rakuten AI" will be "available pre-bundled on HP's consumer and commercial devices in Japan" starting next year. "Rakuten AI" is described as "a next-generation agentic AI platform designed to enhance productivity, support smarter decision-making, and enable intuitive human-AI collaboration" in the release.

Current "Rakuten AI" Applications

The company has been utilizing AI agents internally since July.

New Alliance With HP

RKUNY's future financial and stock price performances might be boosted by this development.

If HPQ's users are satisfied with the new AI PCs, this serves as a validation of the firm's "Rakuten AI." In the future, it could possibly build new income streams pertaining to the monetization of its AI agents.

RKUNY may also be able to command a more demanding valuation, if "Rakuten AI's" success makes it a play on the AI investment theme.

You Should Watch These Risks

The IPO of "Rakuten Card" might be delayed or even aborted, assuming that capital market conditions are unfavorable.

HPQ's devices powered by "Rakuten AI" may be deemed to be inferior to other competing products. That will make it difficult for RKUNY to explore other third-party AI-related deals.

My Concluding Remarks

RKUNY's existing "Buy" rating is retained.

The stock's valuations are appealing compared to its Japanese peer.

The forward EV/EBITDAs for Rakuten and SoftBank Corp. (OTCPK:SOBKY) (OTCPK:SFBQF) (9434.T) are 6.8x and 9.3x, respectively, as per S&P Capital IQ.

Its EBITDA ratio can possibly re-rate by +37% (9.3/6.8), with SOBKY as a benchmark.

My opinion of its corporate moves and 3Q25 outperformance is favorable, which supports a re-rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why This Selloff Looks More Like A Reset Than A Reversal
data.description:
Market Outlook

There’s a point that each bull cycle normally gets to where prices and sentiment stretch a bit too far away from the truth. This two-week period has definitely been that time. High-beta tech stocks imploded, AI leaders stumbled, and the indexes ticked low enough to make news but not break anything of substance.

I think that exactly what the market needed was a reset, such as happened with the S&P 500 (SP500) (SPX) (SPY) (VOO) and Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND). The good news is that it’s accomplished the very things that a reset could be expected to achieve: it’s brought prices back to a sensible level and cleared the fervor of the AI bubble. Further advancement into the end of the year is thus poised to be much healthier.

The Anatomy of the Pullback: The Reason Behind the Need of Excess to Cool Off Before the Flow of Money Back

Corrections do not normally come with convenient answers, and this one came no differently. The markets have been moving for several months, and much of that action has been among the stocks that notched the biggest gains during the AI-capital bubble.

The government shutdown pushed the numbers into a deep freeze, and the market suddenly found itself functioning without the macroeconomic lifeblood that it had become accustomed to having. Investors and policymakers were both left seeing the world through a veil of confusion that could have produced a volatile reaction regardless of the circumstances.

However, the sell-off itself never had anything to do with fundamentals. It’s a matter of pockets that formed after a very hot period. The mega-cap tech stocks pushed prices into a zone where there’s only room for perfect trading, and any kind of imbalance set the nerves of the traders twitching. The sell-off pressured the most extended parts of the markets to take a breather. What’s even stranger is that such a pressure release is often a sign of robust markets and not fragile ones. Broken markets do not adjust through measured steps. They seize, panic, and dry up. None of the above occurred.

What happened instead was a shift of the kind that’s been long overdue. Money slid out of the high fliers and into the financials, energy stocks, industrials, and a few groups within the healthcare sector that have been left alone all year. This is the kind of shift that’s positive. It’s a sign of institutions moving funds around and not abandoning ship. I view this sort of shift as the kind of shift that builds a bull market rather than undermines it.

The AI Trade Didn’t Break

The common story of the pullback is that AI is “cooling off” and that the demand for AI infrastructure finally hit a wall. I do not find that explanation believable at all. What really happened is much simpler. The expectations were running ahead of the proof-of-concept ramp. The hyperscalers spending is still high, and the GPU demand remains incredibly robust. The people building the underlying infrastructure are not moderating the language.

What broke was the sentiment, not the fundamentals. Yet sharp corrections after parabolic moves are a normal feature every time a bull market erupts. Cyclical corrections force a readjustment of thinking. They weed out the timid participants. They also serve as a harsh reminder that the best stocks cannot go up in a straight line. I strongly feel that the AI trade is entering a phase where the trade becomes euphoric and then selective. That’s a much healthier way of compounding.

The Fed’s Ambiguity Was an Overreaction, Rather Than a Macro Turning Point

What’s so odd about the sell-off is the aggressiveness of the pricing of the probability of a December rate cut. At one point, the odds were near 96%. Then, after a couple of weeks of no data, the odds were reduced below 50%. I think that has more to do with confusion than change. The truth is that when policymakers aren’t armed with data, the default position is fear. That doesn’t translate to a hawkish shift.

There’s been no re-emergence of inflation concerns. The labor market, although remaining robust, has been relenting slightly. Moreover, the distortions caused by the government shutdown are such that the immediate macro data that follows will be extremely noisy. The Fed understands this. They also understand that a rise in the financial conditions during a data-muddled winter is not a good idea. The implication is that the Fed will retain its easing bias and merely opt to time its next steps through improved data.

This has implications for the psychology of the markets. Investors do not require drastic action for the stocks to calm down. They merely require that the direction of the money policy remain non-constricted. I expect the December period to bring that clarity to the table, even if the rate itself drifts into the first half of 2026. As soon as the Fed realizes that the rate-raising cycle is over, the discount rate that has suppressed the high-growth stocks will ease up. This could be one of the backdoor triggers that will lift the markets into the closing of the year.

Seasonality and Event Timing Create Unusually Attractive Setup through December

There’s a cadence to markets that tends to play out over generations, and seasonality is one of those weird functions that just keeps happening anyway. November and December are two of the best months of the year for the stock market. This is the time of the year when investment funds flow even out, tax-selling stops, and consumer spending provides a psychological lift.

This year, however, brings a twist. The government shutdown ended right when the seasonal window opened. Nvidia’s (NVDA) earnings are right within the timeframe that markets normally start to stabilize. Economic data will reappear after a prolonged period of silence. The Fed will also be having a meeting that will right the course of confusion that has reigned for so long.

The positioning of these catalysts implies that there is a probability distribution that is skewed to the upside. The truth is that the markets bottom not when the bear case is so vocal and the liquidation is so orderly but after the heavy positioning has already been liquidated and the next catalysts are within range. This is exactly what we see today.

This Looks More Like the Pause That Extends a Bull Market Than the Start of a New Downtrend

The temptation during every pullback is to expect the worst. The more I think about the last few weeks, the more I see the opposite. The credit markets are calm. The breadth is improving. The AI mania has finally boiled off to a manageable temperature. And the macro catalysts that matter are right around the corner.

Those that come with the numbers of change rather than decay are opportunity-driven. This one appears to be among them. If Nvidia meets the market’s expectations next week and the Fed adjusts the market’s expectations in December, then a look back at these times might put the correction period into perspective because the market trimmed its excess and positioned itself for the next rise.

The reset has already occurred. What follows will depend on whether the investment community recognizes that the sell-off caused more good than harm.

data.title: Rubrik: Solid Cybersecurity Play At Fair Value
data.description:
Rubrik (RBRK) is a newer and rising player in cybersecurity. It's ben public for little more than a year, so the opportunity is still young. I think there's a good future for the business, but I'm not convinced the current price is a comfortable one at which to buy.

Business Model

Rubrik works in cybersecurity, through a cloud-based, software-as-a-service solution known as Rubrik Security Cloud ("RSC").

RSC offers layered solutions, with four broad categories:

  • Data Protection
  • Data Security Posture Management
  • Data Threat Analytics
  • Cyber Recovery

They describe the overall product offered by these as "Cyber Resilience."

They primarily make revenue from these through subscriptions to these different services, varying according to customers' needs.

Their sales efforts follow a "land and expand" strategy, whereby they acquire customers whose long-term contracts can grow as their own businesses naturally grow. A customer's growth naturally increases use of a current service, while encouraging expansion of the subscription into the other solutions.

As of Q2 FY 2026, they had 2,505 customers with $100,000 or more in Subscription ARR as of July 31, 2025.

Financial History

Founded in 2013 and going public in 2024, Rubrik is still relatively young in its life and has a limited history of publicly available financial data (in fiscal years ended January). That history shows one of revenue growth.

This growth took off more sharply in the same year that they went public.

For the YTD reported results of FY 2026, this sharp revenue growth has continued.

This owes largely to a growing number of customers with subscription ARR above $100K. This is a mixture of recently won accounts as well as previous generations continuing to increase their subscription, consistent with the "land and expand" strategy.

What is important is how this allowed Rubrik to transition from a net cash burner to generating free cash flow. This began in Q1 of FY 2025 and has remained positive since.

They ended the second quarter with about $1.5B in cash and liquid assets, against $1.1B in convertible debt. This debt was issued in June and expires in 2030.

Outlook and Valuation

RBRK currently trades for about $75 per share with a market cap of about $15B. It's not the largest tech stock one could buy, but it isn't tiny either. We need to ask if there's growth and if this price is reasonable for it. Co-founder and CEO Bipul Sinha believes there is much growth ahead, stating in the Q2 earnings call:

Again, very early days for Rubrik. We are in the first decade of our multi-decade story.

The calls discussed the multiple ways they are feeling growth, across their customers and solutions. He summarized the changes as a response to the rapidly evolving situation posed by AI:

...we are in a very large and expanding market of cyber resilience. And as customers are looking to transform their businesses into AI enterprises, they are doing multiple transformations around cloud, around infrastructure and cyber resiliency is the #1 topic for them because if your data doesn't have integrity or availability, none of the AI will be useful or helpful.

Looking at their recently sharp growth and its timing with emergence of generative AI in 2023, I suspect there will be continued opportunity here, as AI continues to be an area of investment in the business world.

Sam Boughedda of Yahoo Finance reported that global AI capex is expected to reach $423B in 2025 and $571B in 2026. As AI capacities continue to grow, cybersecurity needs related to AI will grow as well. Consequently, I think strong, near-term growth remains for RBRK.

With their healthy balance sheet, supported by liquid assets and manageable, convertible debt, this isn't a terribly risky business, and I think their generation of free cash flow eases them from the need to raise additional capital.

For the first six months of FY 2026, they reported a very healthy FCF margin of 15%. With more growth ahead and a capital-light model typical of tech, there's room for this margin to increase with more growth.

We can do a Discounted Cash Flow calculation, using an optimistic FCF CAGR of 25% for this multi-decade journey and use the current guidance for FY 2026 FCF, ranging from $145M to $155M.

Priced for a 10% discount rate (typical return of a market index) that suggests a fair value of $85 per share, for a market cap of about $16.7B. The current price of $75 is a modest discount, but I think there are some risk factors to consider.

One is that a significant portion of this value is in the future, reflected in a Terminal Value of about $13.5B. An investment in RBRK could very easily be upset by disruptions in the growth. For example, suppose that AI capex slows down more than expected in the next 5 - 10 years, as it may prove to be too costly or less value-creative than initially projected. This would naturally slow growth for any players in cybersecurity.

It is also a very competitive industry, and Rubrik is honest about who they consider contenders in their own investor materials. Palo Alto (PANW), which I've also covered, is an example. They have deep relationships with many large accounts. They aren't counting customers by $100K ARRs. To achieve a high FCF CAGR, Rubrik needs to win big accounts like these too, all while incumbents continually innovate and expand relationships with customers too.

I therefore think more margin of safety is warranted before taking a position. An entry price below $60 would more adequately provide this, and so I think RBRK is fairly valued for now.

With Q3 results in a few weeks, updates to the full-year FCF guidance will be important, and any details that speak to their ability to gain customers or grow this FCF margin could improve the valuation.

Conclusion

Cybersecurity is a rapidly evolving industry. Investors can count on a general idea of growth, but the speed and the winners of that growth are necessarily tough to predict. What we can do is look at who is profitable, whose balance sheets are in order, and whose stock is priced for some undervaluation. RBRK doesn't seem to meet the latter at the moment, but isn't terribly risky at this price either. For that reason, I rate the stock a Hold.

data.title: Coty: Too Much Bad News Priced In - Initiating With A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

As Coty Inc. (COTY) changes up its organizational structure, conducts a strategic review of 30% of its business, an attempt to monetize its stake in Wella and the expected loss of its Gucci license, I think the company’s disappointing results for Q1’26 almost feels like an afterthought. Indeed, Coty reported a quarter roughly in line with expectations for the first time in a year, and it expects sales next quarter to decline at the more favorable end of the prior range. Still, I believe it is a steep hill to climb for sales to grow in second half of FY’26, particularly with the Consumer Beauty business in such a state of flux. On the earnings call, comments around the color cosmetics business could take longer to turnaround with an outright sale far from a given. Despite all the uncertainty and negative news, I think the stock appears cheap enough to justify a ‘buy’ rating.

Recent Results

It’s no secret that Coty has had to deal with the Gucci license wind-down and a still-challenged consumer-beauty segment. That said, during the quarter, the company beat on revenue by $218 million with revenues coming in at $1.58 billion. On a like-for-like basis, that’s 8% lower compared to last year. By segment, North American sales fell 6% with both EMEA and Asia Pacific dropping 9%.

When we look at what led to the results this quarter, we can see that the Prestige business was down 6% on a like-for-like basis with Consumer Beauty, particularly mass color cosmetics, weighing on the top line.

Prestige fragrances grew mid-single digits in sell-out terms with the U.S. market up 7% and Coty tracking right in line with that strength. EMEA prestige fragrances remained positive but slowed to low-single digits largely because of a soft French market whereas the U.K. and Spain stayed firm at mid-single digits. Asia and travel retail also contributed meaningfully with China prestige fragrance sell-out growing roughly twice the market rate.

Moving down the income statement, gross margins were down 100bps year over year because of a 40bps headwind on tariffs. However, more worrying was on EBITDA as the SG&A line increased with promotional intensity increasing. As such, EBITDA clocked in at $291 million for a margin of 18.8%, reflecting a 270bps drop.

Gross margin expanded nicely year over year thanks to pricing discipline, limited promotional intensity, and favorable mix toward higher-margin ultra-premium and prestige launches. Adjusted EBITDA margins held up well despite the top-line pressure which tells me the cost discipline and operating leverage in the core prestige business remain intact. Adjusted earnings per share landed ahead of consensus reflecting the margin tailwind and lower interest expense after recent refinancing activity. Free cash flow stayed solid and the balance sheet ended the quarter in good shape with net debt roughly unchanged sequentially and leverage comfortably inside the company’s long-term target range of below 2 times even after factoring in the share buyback activity that continued during the period. Liquidity remains ample with over a billion dollars of cash and available revolver capacity.

Another negative (which has been well known for some time) was that the Gucci license termination upon natural expiry removes a meaningful but not dominant piece of the mix. However, Coty is suing Kering and Gucci in the U.K. over the upcoming transfer of Gucci's beauty and fragrance license, which Coty currently holds until 2028. Kering's deal with L’Oréal granting a 50-year exclusive license for Gucci beauty products starting after Coty's license ends, violates existing contractual rights according to Coty.

On the earnings call, management reiterated that from a portfolio standpoint, the company reiterated that no single brand exceeds approximately 10% of total sales and that roughly 85% of the beauty portfolio is now either owned perpetual or secured under very long-term licenses of more than seven years remaining. On the bright side, with that overhang lifted, Coty can now fully lean into Burberry, Chloé, Hugo Boss, Marc Jacobs, and the fast-emerging ultra-premium collections.

Management also confirmed the ongoing strategic review of the global consumer-beauty color cosmetics business under Gordon von Bretten with a focus on profitability and cash generation while separately noting that the profitable Brazil consumer-beauty operation of around $400 million in sales is likely to reach a ‘conclusion’ sooner.

Finally, despite the share price decline, I think the balance sheet looks to be in okay shape. As shown in the two images below, Coty has some $4.0 billion of long-term debt against a cash position of $265 million. With $1.0 billion of EBITDA for the LTM period, net leverage is roughly 3.8x and interest coverage is 5x with interest expense for the LTM period at $199 million (source: Bloomberg).

With the long-term issuer rating for Coty at Ba1 by Moody's (stable) and BB+ by S&P with negative outlook, the company doesn’t have high-grade ratings but it’s strong enough to get the firm a relatively low cost of capital and access to debt capital markets. As well, most maturities are laddered, reducing the immediate refinancing risk so that Coty can refinance as its bonds come due.

Strategic review thoughts

On the conference call post quarter, it was interesting to see CEO Sue Nabi react to media reports suggesting the Luxury business may be for sale, mentioning this as "delusional" and "categorically false." To me, this makes sense given how Coty has shifted its tone since the beginning of this year to doubling down on its fragrance efforts, including taking advantage of attractive structural tailwinds, growing across price points, and expanding into scenting adjacencies such as body mists. On Consumer Beauty, it was never obvious to the market how launching a strategic review would drive "renewed momentum and sharper focus" for the division as described in the initial press release in late September.

Given the commentary around the Brazil business, I think it’s increasingly likely that the strategic review will focus around a sale of this business rather than turning it around. In addition, given that it’s taken a while on the color cosmetics side, there has probably been limited interest from financial or strategic buyers such that Coty will focus its efforts on turning around this business, which could take considerable time. On the color cosmetics side, it was also hinted at that there’s more work to be done to improve the cost structure, which could take 12-18 months or longer, so I wouldn’t say a sale is imminent for this business.

Gucci dispute

Overall, I’m of the view that Coty needs to optimize the Gucci brand in a more tactical way while focusing on accelerating the rest of its portfolio. Within the terms of the license, Coty will continue to support the brand in terms of maintaining a specific targeted level of brand investment. But even then, this is definitely not a situation that Coty wants to be in, as growing the Gucci brand over the next two years would mean creating an even bigger hole to fill. Based on my estimation, it’s likely that the Gucci business could be 11% of total EBITDA.

Regardless of what happens with Gucci, I don’t think investors should count on Coty winning business here any more. At the end of 2028, we would have seen this part of the business evaporate anyways. What’s more, the portfolio outside Gucci is strong with a significant portion of revenues coming from prestige fragrances and prestige-adjacent skincare with virtually no single-brand risk and a cost base that has been reset lower. I’d wager that combination should drive meaningful operating leverage and free-cash-flow growth once the top line stabilizes.

Outlook and risks

In my view, the February print will be the first real proof point that the prestige fragrance engine is fully back on track after a year of destocking noise. Management already guided Q2 LFL to the better end of the prior -3% to -5% range and explicitly called out a “good holiday season” driven by continued U.S. mid-single-digit category growth and the full rollout of BOSS Bottled Beyond which is already ranking top-three in several big European markets.

I feel the early read on holiday gifting will be crucial because nearly half of annual prestige fragrance dollars historically ring in Q4 and any sign that Coty is finally growing sell-out ahead of the market again would confirm the U.S. recovery is real and sustainable heading into calendar 2026. That said, while certainly easier comps play a factor, outside of the US prestige business, sell-out trends are still underperforming the beauty market.

In terms of the risks, the biggest risk to my thesis remains execution on the consumer-beauty turnaround and the timing and multiple received of any Brazil divestiture. Color cosmetics is still losing share in most major markets and the strategic review could drag longer than expected or end with a value-destructive outcome if Coty decides to keep a chronically low-margin business that continues to dilute group returns. Ultimately, a fast sale at a reasonable multiple of the Brazil business would help Coty get cash in the door and pay down some debt and so I think this should be something in the cards to watch for in 2026.

Outside of these risks, another risk is on the macro side, which to some extent is outside of the company’s control. As potential investors in Coty, I think its worth pointing out that these are luxury products at the end of the day. Fragrances in particular have been remarkably resilient for years but if gifting weakens more than expected this holiday season or for a prolonged period during a recession would undoubtedly be a negative to the story.

Finally, the litigation with Kering carries downside. Litigation is expensive and while likely in the millions compared to EBITDA in the hundreds of millions, an unfavorable outcome would imply lower EBITDA in 2026-28 until when the contract would expire naturally.

Valuation

On valuation, Coty trades pretty inexpensively. For a $7.6 billion enterprise value company on a business that can likely grow its top and bottom line at least 2% per year (assuming zero margin expansion), Coty trades for just 7.3x forward EBITDA (source: Bloomberg).

On a P/E basis, the company trades for 8.2x P/E. As shown below, on both P/E and EV/EBITDA, Coty trades well below peer group multiples at 23.8x P/E and 11.4x EV/EBITDA (source: Bloomberg). Overall, I think this discount is unwarranted as even assuming a 10-12% hit on EBITDA from the loss of the Gucci contract, the company would trade at a major discount.

Given where the stock trades now, down almost 47% year to date, most analysts have yet to upgrade their ratings on the stock, with 4 ‘buy’ ratings, 15 ‘hold’ ratings, and 2 ‘sell’ ratings. But given that the valuation has already discounted more than a fair share of bad news, I think that shares offer up good value to investors today.

Takeaway

Despite the barrage of near-term challenges with the impending Gucci license loss, a protracted strategic review of Consumer Beauty, persistent share erosion in mass color cosmetics, and ongoing litigation with Kering, I think the core business is solid and investors have more than discounted the negatives into the stock’s valuation.

From a risk perspective, Coty is nowhere near bankruptcy risk given that the leverage is manageable, with net leverage around 3.8×, well-laddered maturities, and ample liquidity. Even with some slight deterioration in margins year over year, cost discipline is holding firm and the portfolio is now overwhelmingly composed of owned assets or very long-term licenses, with concentration risk meaningfully reduced. Trading at just 7.3x forward EV/EBITDA and 8.2x P/E, I think the multiple could rerate rather quickly on any combination of a clean Brazil divestiture, modest stabilization in color cosmetics, or clear evidence of sustained prestige outperformance during the holidays or as management executes in 2026. For investors comfortable with some execution and litigation uncertainty, Coty currently represents one of the most attractive risk/reward opportunities I can find in the market today. Long Coty.

data.title: SRV: Energy Infrastructure Delivers The Goods
data.description:
Over the past two to three years, the midstream energy sector has begun to rebound from a disastrous run that started about ten years ago. Back in February Franklin Templeton, sponsors of the ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity Fund (EMO), wrote an insight suggesting that global energy demands fuel the growth outlook for the midstream energy sector. That insight includes a graphic showing the ten-year decline in the midstream energy markets which have led to some attractive current valuations.

Meanwhile, midstream energy valuations remain attractive, in our view. For example, using EV/EBITDA as one valuation metric, the Alerian MLP Index is still trading at modest multiples, especially compared to its long-term history (Exhibit 1).

Also in February of this year I reviewed the NXG NextGen Infrastructure Income Fund (NXG), which I rated a Buy at the time due to its strong holdings in energy infrastructure and power delivery companies that stand to benefit from the growing global demand for electricity. Top holdings in NXG such as Energy Transfer (ET) are benefiting from the explosive demand for AI data centers and natural gas needed to help supply the power grid to meet that growing demand.

The final takeaway from the recent ET earnings call summarizes the impacts of this growing demand:

Energy Transfer’s third quarter call emphasized disciplined capital allocation and a robust pipeline of long-term, high-return projects, many of which are now fully contracted with investment-grade counterparties. Management projects $4.6 billion in organic growth capital for 2025 and expects continued growth in natural gas demand, driven by multi-decade agreements with data centers and utilities. While guidance was revised to slightly below the previous range, the company reported volume records and detailed significant progress on major expansions and strategic partnerships, reinforcing its position as a premier provider in the evolving U.S. energy infrastructure landscape.

SRV: Capitalizing on Midstream Energy

The sibling fund to NXG from NXG Investment Management is the NXG Cushing® Midstream Energy Fund (SRV). If you are not yet familiar with the two funds, you may want to read about SRV in my first review of that CEF (closed end fund) that I initially rated a Strong Buy in April 2023, shortly after the fund changed its name and investment approach. The fund announced in January 2023 that effective April 3 it would change its name from the Cushing MLP & Infrastructure Total Return fund. The change from MLP & Infrastructure to Midstream is a subtle change and further detail is included in the announcement.

Effective as of April 3, 2023, the Fund will pursue its investment objective by investing, under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its net assets, plus any borrowings for investment purposes, in a portfolio of midstream energy investments. The Fund considers midstream energy investments to be investments that offer economic exposure to securities of midstream energy companies, which are companies that engage provide midstream services in the energy infrastructure sector, including the gathering, transporting, processing, fractionation, storing, refining and distribution of natural resources, such as natural gas, natural gas liquids, crude oil refined petroleum products, biofuels, carbon sequestration, solar, and wind. The Fund considers a company to be a midstream energy company if at least 50% of its assets, income, sales or profits are committed to, derived from or otherwise related to midstream energy services.

I have since reviewed SRV two additional times, most recently rating the fund a Buy. After digesting the recent news of an upcoming RO (rights offering), along with a special distribution to satisfy the requirement to distribute excess capital gains, I maintain my Buy rating on SRV.

Before I get into the details of the RO and the special distribution, I want to briefly review the fund, its top holdings, and recent performance relative to peers.

From the fund website, here are a few key facts about SRV. Note that there are currently about $200M in AUM (assets under management), and the fund currently trades near par at a small discount of -0.3%. The fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.45 which offers a current distribution rate of about 12.5%.
